# Questions in Livestream 176

Live Stream #176: The People Groups of YouTube

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=494" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:14</a> | In your novels, do you focus more on world-building or on character? | He aims for a balance, but says his fiction usually leans more toward strong character work. | He says fiction needs both, especially because his own novels are mostly science fiction and require solid world-building. Still, he enjoys writing strong characters most, and he points to pacing, dialogue, characterization, and a mix of classic and contemporary science fiction influences as shaping what he likes in fiction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=718" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:58</a> | Are angels, powers, and spirit creatures also children of God, and does God love them as he loves humanity? | He says they are God's spiritual creatures and are loved, but not in the same redemptive way as humans. | He answers yes in the sense that angels, powers, principalities, and other spiritual creatures belong to God's spiritual creation. But he distinguishes that from God's redemptive love toward humanity: obedient spirit creatures remain in God's love, while rebellious ones fall out of that relationship, and God is not obligated to go the same extra redemptive mile with them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=838" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">13:58</a> | Can Christian authors write mythic fiction about angels, origins, and paradise using non-biblical imagery? | Yes, provided the theology and worldview stay straight and the fiction does not contradict biblical revelation. | He says Christian fiction can use mythic or non-biblical imagery around angels, origins, paradise, or similar themes if the author keeps the theological framework straight. He uses C. S. Lewis's science fiction as an example of biblical concepts being recast imaginatively without contradicting biblical revelation, even though Lewis's fiction is not personally to his taste. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=1077" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:57</a> | What do you think of the Catholic Church's process for verifying miracle claims? | He likes the caution and says the process tends to avoid declaring certainty too quickly. | He says the Catholic process is cautious in a way he appreciates: the church may reject a claim, find it unsubstantiated, or say it finds no objection to treating it as miraculous, but it generally avoids stamping the event as certainly a miracle. He also notes a tension with canonization, where miracle evidence is used for saints, and says he would like the same caution applied consistently and reflected more in ordinary church life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=1629" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:09</a> | What are your thoughts on Michael Heiser's book *Angels*? | He has only skimmed it, but says it did not seem as strong as *The Unseen Realm*. | He is careful not to overstate the judgment because he has only skimmed the book rather than read it closely. From that limited exposure, he says it did not seem to reach the level of *The Unseen Realm*, which he does recommend, and he has heard similar reservations about Heiser's demon book. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=1694" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:14</a> | How do you resolve the tension between theological retrieval and Heiser's claim that the Bible's context is not church history? | He distinguishes christotelic reading from later christocentric retrieval and says the original context should remain the starting point. | He says both context and tradition can be useful, but they should be ordered properly. Start with the biblical and ancient context to get the exegesis right, then bring in later tradition to see where it agrees, adds helpful layers, or needs reevaluation; tradition is not dismissed, but it should not replace the original context as the starting point. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=2252" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:32</a> | How do we know a Dead Sea Scroll fragment is the lost Book of the Wars of Yahweh? | He is uncertain, but recalls that matching quotations in other texts may have been used to identify it. | He repeatedly qualifies the answer as hazy memory and outside his field. As he recalls it, quotations from the Book of the Wars in other texts matched a scroll found among the Qumran materials, which may be how the identification was made, but he says he could be wrong and does not claim expertise on the Dead Sea Scrolls. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=2512" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:52</a> | If blessings are a big deal, why do we bless food? | He says it is really a prayer of thanks rather than literally making the food holy. | He says the meal prayer commonly called blessing food is more accurately thanksgiving to God, not an act that makes the food holy. He distinguishes that usage from blessing a person and notes that language such as "blessed are you, O Lord" belongs to a different category than consecrating or empowering an object. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=2848" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:28</a> | What are your thoughts on Keil and Delitzsch's Exodus commentary? | He is not a fan, especially where it handles Egyptian material poorly. | He says it is not the worst commentary he has used, but he does not think it is a good one, especially when it comments on Egyptian matters. He gives the location of Ramesses as an example of an Egyptological mistake, then recommends treating commentaries as starting points, consulting several, and beginning with Nahum Sarna for Exodus before comparing others. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=3094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:34</a> | Where did the myth that Job is the oldest biblical book come from? | He is not sure, but says serious scholars no longer treat Job as the oldest book. | He says he does not know the origin of the claim, though he associates it with more traditional or fundamentalist teaching. He rejects it because Job's internal context points to the Iron Age, including references such as Arameans and Chaldeans and a setting that must be after the Mitanni period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=3342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:42</a> | Is partialism only a heresy if you believe in divine simplicity? | He says divine simplicity and the essence-energies distinction are not necessarily mutually exclusive, though divine simplicity has other issues. | He says divine simplicity and the essence-energies distinction can coexist if simplicity is applied to the divine essence. Partialism can be a problem for both frameworks, but he thinks divine simplicity has an additional difficulty because it can press toward dissolving identity, which would undermine hypostasis, persons, and therefore the Trinity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=3485" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:05</a> | What does John Walton mean by functional creation in Genesis? | He says Walton reads Genesis as presenting creation in terms of function and form rather than material origins alone. | He explains Walton as reading Genesis 1-2 through a temple-cosmology model where the text is concerned with creation's function, order, and form rather than a scientific account of material origins. He agrees that Genesis is not asking modern scientific questions, but adds that functional creation does not exclude the reality of material creation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=4083" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:03</a> | Are the Horites in Genesis 14:6 Hurrians, or is this an anachronism? | He says Horites and Hurrians are different groups; the Horites are tied to Mount Seir and possibly the Shasu. | He distinguishes several similarly named groups: Horites are associated with Mount Seir/Edom and may relate to Shasu; Hurrians are farther north, tied to Syria and the remains of the Mitanni world; Hivites are in north-central Canaan near Dan and Kadesh; and Hittites are Anatolian. On that basis, he does not treat Horites as simply Hurrians. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=4395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:15</a> | Could first-century Judeans have spoken Greek, and why are some sayings preserved in other languages? | He says ancient polyglotism was normal, especially in crossroads communities. | He argues that multilingualism was far more normal in ancient crossroads regions than it is for many modern English speakers. Greek had been present in Judea for centuries after Alexander, the temple warning inscription assumes basic Greek literacy, and comparable multilingualism appears in places where trade, migration, and imperial contact intersect. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=4660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:40</a> | How can scholars better fight misinformation and non-scholarly myths online? | He says the channel spends much of its time doing exactly that, though the problem can feel overwhelming. | He says misinformation is one of the channel's regular targets, naming examples such as Ron Wyatt claims, ancient aliens, flat-earth material, and similar online myths. He also argues that YouTube and Google amplify the problem because algorithmic bubbles feed users what keeps them engaged, including oversimplifications or lies, since the platform is driven by advertising revenue rather than truth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=4981" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:01</a> | Is Moloch an ancient demonic god or a ritual fire sacrifice? | He says the latter is more likely, because evidence for Moloch as a worshiped deity is lacking. | He says older interpretation treated Moloch as a deity because of the link with the Semitic word for king, but current evidence points more toward a burnt child-sacrifice practice. His reason is that Moloch does not appear where a deity normally would, such as seals, ritual texts, or other evidence of worship, despite many Levantine seal impressions being known. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=5126" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:26</a> | What do you think about the tradition that John survived being plunged into boiling oil? | He says it could explain John's exile, but the solidity of the tradition is hard to determine. | He says the tradition could make sense of why John was exiled rather than executed, since surviving boiling oil would have been frightening to authorities. But he does not claim the story is established: it appears in early historians, yet the reliability of that tradition is uncertain, and other explanations for exile are also possible. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=5230" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:10</a> | Was Muhammad given a false revelation by a demon, or was he lying? | He avoids speculating directly and says Islamic revelation contradicts biblical revelation. | He explicitly declines to speculate because Islam is outside his expertise and because he still has professional reasons to be cautious about working in Egypt. The limited claim he is willing to make is that the Quran and the Bible contradict each other, so both cannot function together as harmonious sacred revelation; he points the questioner to someone more specialized in Islam. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=5372" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:32</a> | What is the biblically accurate process of getting married? | He says there is no prescriptive process, though Israelite marriage involved legal and contractual negotiation. | He says Scripture describes ancient practices rather than prescribing one required wedding process. In Israelite first marriages, the parents typically arranged a legal contract, the groom prepared a house, the bride's family hosted the wedding, the couple spent the first night at the bride's parents' house, and then the bride moved into the house the groom had prepared. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=5749" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:49</a> | Were ancient marriage consummation customs a throwback to fertility pagan rites? | He says no; he treats them as pragmatic and contractual safeguards. | He says the custom was not a pagan fertility throwback but a practical and contractual safeguard for both families. Consummation completed the marriage, and the evidence surrounding it could protect the bride's family if the groom later disputed the marriage or the bride's virginity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=5900" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:20</a> | What if a woman broke her hymen before marriage through sports or another cause? | He says that was less of an issue in the ancient world because women were not usually playing sports in that way. | He gives a very limited answer: in the ancient setting he is describing, women generally were not engaged in the kinds of sports activity assumed by the question, so this would not have been as common a practical problem. The transcript then moves quickly into a different question. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=6660" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:00</a> | Is an upside-down cross evil? | No. He says it is traditionally the cross of St. Peter and originally symbolized humility, not evil. | He says the upside-down cross is the cross of St. Peter and was historically associated with Peter's humility in not wanting to be crucified like Christ. Its use by Satanists is, in his view, an appropriation or inversion of a Christian symbol, not the original meaning of the symbol itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=7249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:49</a> | Would you commission an official Dr. Falk chronology-of-the-Exodus infographic poster? | He is doubtful because the merch store gets little traffic and posters have not sold well. | He says the idea is not impossible, but it is hard to justify as a standalone project because the merch store gets little traffic and previous items have barely sold. He suggests an infographic would make more sense after work on an Exodus book or related graphics, when the images and data would already exist and the extra effort would be smaller. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=8456" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:56</a> | Did any nation other than Israel have prophecies about unbelieving nations being blessed through them? | He says he does not know of a close parallel and treats Israel as unusual on that point. | He contrasts Israel with Egypt and Mesopotamia, where foreign nations were normally viewed as enemies, outsiders, chaos, or barbarism rather than peoples to be reconciled through the nation. He still qualifies Israel's own view as complex, because Israel could see foreign nations both as objects of blessing or evangelization and as aggressors who would not all be reconciled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=8708" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:08</a> | How should we use sources like Nuzi and Amarna responsibly in exegesis? | He says to use them as historical sources with caution, accounting for genre, rhetoric, and possible hyperbole. | He says Nuzi, Amarna, and biblical texts should all be used as historical sources in context, not flattened or accepted uncritically. He gives the Amarna letters as a major source for the 14th-century Levant and the Amorite takeover, which then helps illuminate why Israel later encounters Amorite strongholds, while also warning that ancient texts include rhetoric and hyperbole. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=9419" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:36:59</a> | How do practitioners of Orthodox Judaism usually interpret the seed promise in Genesis 22:18? | He says they usually read the seed as the nation of Israel rather than as a messianic individual. | He says Orthodox Jewish interpretation typically reads the "seed" in Genesis 22:18 corporately as Israel, similar to how it reads the seed of the woman in Genesis 3:15. Within that hermeneutical model, the blessing of the nations is tied to Israel, and he says this reading is straightforwardly coherent within that framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=9552" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:12</a> | What do you think of the age variations in Genesis 5 and 11 between the Septuagint, Samaritan Pentateuch, and Masoretic Text? | He first clarifies the textual abbreviations and treats the issue as a textual-tradition question. | He identifies the Masoretic Text, Septuagint, and Samaritan Pentateuch as different text families whose numbers produce different totals. He cautions against simply adding the timelines together, noting that biblical number texts are often under textual stress, and says each age or numerical variant has to be evaluated case by case rather than solved by one blanket preference. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=10521" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:21</a> | What does "righteous" mean in the Old Testament, for example in Genesis 18:23? | He says it does not simply mean sinless, but concerns a broader covenantal and moral category. | He says righteousness in Genesis 18 does not mean merely that someone has personally never committed a sin. In his explanation, Old Testament thought includes inherited and accumulated sin debt across generations, analogous to family financial debts, so righteousness concerns one's standing under that wider moral burden rather than simple individual sinlessness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=11915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:35</a> | Is there proof the peace symbol was ever meant for demonic use? | He says no, describing it instead as a long-lived symbol later misused by others. | He says not in ancient times: the symbol sometimes called a broken cross in a circle had earlier non-demonic uses, including in medieval contexts and even on Bible covers. He compares it to other symbols that later cultures repurposed, arguing that later misuse does not define the original meaning of the symbol. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=12789" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:09</a> | Which king was the Exodus pharaoh if Ramesses II's mummy is in a museum? | He still identifies Ramesses II, saying the Bible does not require the pharaoh to have drowned in the sea. | He identifies Ramesses II based on toponyms, the abandonment of Avaris in the 19th Dynasty, and the Merneptah Stele's dating. The existence of Ramesses II's mummy is not a problem for him because he argues the biblical text does not say Pharaoh drowned; he attributes that idea to later tradition and a traditional reading of Psalm 136 rather than to the Exodus narrative itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=13009" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:49</a> | Why are unmarried couples who have sex sinful, and how does this show marriage is more than a legal certificate? | He says it violates marriage's covenantal purpose and can function as a mock or test marriage. | He frames the issue under the command against adultery, arguing that sex outside marriage can wrong a future spouse because there is no covenantal guarantee of permanence. He also says it imitates marriage without the leaving-and-cleaving commitment of Genesis, making a mockery of the created order and showing that marriage is theological, not merely legal paperwork. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=13147" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:07</a> | Why was the battle over the body of Moses left out of the Old Testament? | He says it is an ideological or heavenly-realm event, not a Torah narrative event. | He says the battle over Moses' body is not presented as a time-and-space event in the Torah narrative but as something belonging to the heavenly or ideological realm, requiring prophetic perspective to know about it. Moses still dies in the Torah, and the later dispute concerns possession or status after death rather than an event the Torah needed to narrate directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=13332" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:12</a> | How has the Christian faith been good for the world? | He gives examples including women's consent in marriage and periods of social peace and institution-building. | He gives a rapid list rather than a full apologetic argument: Christian marriage vows made room for a woman's yes or no, Christian influence limited practices such as gladiatorial games, human sacrifice, and certain wartime cruelties, and Christian institutions fostered hospitals, charity, orphanages, literacy, universities, and conditions in which science could flourish. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=13674" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:54</a> | Could a body found in an Egyptian tomb have been a later local or tomb robber rather than the original burial? | Yes. He says locals sometimes reused tombs for unrelated burials, so later intrusion is possible. | He says tomb contents are not always original to the burial, because locals sometimes placed unrelated dead in accessible tombs or bodies could be moved, unwrapped, and discarded in pits. He cites the tomb of Nefertari as an example where unrelated skeletons were found, and says tomb pits often collect material not originally belonging to the tomb. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=14425" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:00:25</a> | What are your thoughts on Christian nationalism? | He strongly rejects it, defining it as a distorted mix of Old Testament law-as-state-law and racism rather than ordinary Christian civic duty. | He defines Christian nationalism narrowly, not as ordinary Christian civic engagement or conservatism, but as judaizing Old Testament law into state law with racism added. He rejects it as unchristian and totalitarian because it prioritizes the state, controls morality and speech, and in his view resembles other nationalist ideologies in dangerous ways. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=15509" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:18:29</a> | Were there Freemasons on the King James Bible editing board? | He says the verifiable history of Freemasonry is too late to make that likely. | He says the earliest verifiable Freemasonry record he finds is an initiation in 1646, later than the 1611 KJV, so he thinks Freemasons on the board are unlikely despite broader Masonic claims. He adds that even if an unbeliever or morally compromised person worked on a translation, the work still has to be judged by its scholarly quality and purpose, not merely by the translator's character. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/sKKScMy3Q64?t=15841" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:24:01</a> | How can you have confidence as a Christian? | He says doubt remains part of Christian life, but Scripture, prayer, and faithful church practice can steady and reduce it. | He says Christians should not expect all doubt to vanish, but they can mitigate and minimize it through ordinary faithfulness. His practical counsel is to keep reading Scripture for study, learning, and devotion; keep praying and talking to God; stay connected to a healthy church and Christian community; and avoid controversies and tangents that do not strengthen faith. |
